text:
Overview
========

This document describes the Hallucination Stress Test Suite (HSTS) and an associated Recovery Playbook for operationalizing detection, triage, and automated mitigation of hallucination regressions across Redwood’s inference fleet (hosted, dedicated, private). The HSTS is intended to be integrated with the eval-mesh and probe-orchestrator pipelines to provide deterministic and stochastic stress probes that surface hallucination risk under real-world conditions: prompt priming changes, quantized kernels, KV-cache boundary events, and latency-preserving batching.

Goals
-----
- Provide a repeatable, versioned set of probes that exercise hallucination-prone behaviors.
- Define measurable metrics and pass/fail thresholds for gating rollouts.
- Prescribe triage steps and automated repair actions (prompt rewrites, quant-fallback, enhanced priming) to reduce incident MTTR.
...

text:
Summary

Purpose:
This document defines an operational SLA (service-level agreement) that bridges our internal evaluation harness, prompt/benchmark suites, model onboarding checks, quality guardrails, and regression-triage procedures for quantized inference artifacts. The objective is to create a single, measurable contract for acceptable "fidelity delta" when migrating models or deploying mixed-precision/quantized variants into production.

Audience and scope:
- Applied ML engineers, runtime owners, SREs, and product owners responsible for model promotion and inference economics.
- Applies to all Redwood-managed serving paths (hosted, dedicated, private) where quantization or mixed-precision artifacts are used.
- Covers: pre-promotion evals, shadow validation, continuous monitoring, alerting thresholds, and rollback actions.

Definitions:
- Fidelity delta: the measured change in anch...

title: Runtime Shadow Rehearsal and Localized Probe Synthesis Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines the runtime "shadow rehearsal" pattern: an operationalized workflow that (1) synthesizes localized evaluation probes targeted at tenant cohorts, locales, and latency bands; (2) runs those probes in a low-risk shadow mesh against quantized and mixed-precision artifacts; and (3) performs...
text:
Summary

This playbook defines the runtime "shadow rehearsal" pattern: an operationalized workflow that (1) synthesizes localized evaluation probes targeted at tenant cohorts, locales, and latency bands; (2) runs those probes in a low-risk shadow mesh against quantized and mixed-precision artifacts; and (3) performs cost-aware rehearsal passes that validate quality, latency, and coherence before a real rollout. The goal is to detect regressions and quant-induced degradations earlier while bounding evaluation cost and preserving tenant privacy.

Scope

- Applies to model promotion and quantized rollouts for text generation and multi-turn chat services (service names: eval-orch, shadow-mesh, rehearsal-runner).
- Covers probe generation, seed selection, rehearsal execution, gating metrics, and rollback triggers.
- Intended for on-prem, private VPC, and hosted Dedicated deployments.

Why ...

title: Eval Sprint Orchestration and Annotator Rota
preview:
Summary:
This runbook defines the lightweight sprint-oriented orchestration model we use to deliver targeted eval work (bug-hunting, quant checks, prompt stress runs, and slice-specific labeling) with a short turnaround. It combines a deterministic scheduler in the eval harness, an annotator rota (part-time reviewer...
text:
Summary:
This runbook defines the lightweight sprint-oriented orchestration model we use to deliver targeted eval work (bug-hunting, quant checks, prompt stress runs, and slice-specific labeling) with a short turnaround. It combines a deterministic scheduler in the eval harness, an annotator rota (part-time reviewers/labelers), and a distributed telemetry gate so results can be promoted into the model-onboarding pipeline or trigger regression triage.

Goals:
- Deliver small, high-impact eval scopes in 2–5 business day sprints.
- Minimize lead time between discovery (telemetry alert) and annotated evidence for action.
- Keep annotator overhead low with clear instructions and a rotating, documented rota.
- Ensure traceability from samples -> annotations -> evaluation artifacts in the eval harness.

Scope:
This document covers: sprint cadence and roles, prioritization criteria, annotator...

title: Few-shot priming economics and sample compression guide
preview:
Summary:
This guide documents Redwood's recommended approach to designing few-shot prompts and compressed sample sets for production evals. It balances quality (coherence, instruction-following) against unit-costs (tokens per probe, annotation overhead) and operational constraints (throughput, latency for streaming ...
text:
Summary:
This guide documents Redwood's recommended approach to designing few-shot prompts and compressed sample sets for production evals. It balances quality (coherence, instruction-following) against unit-costs (tokens per probe, annotation overhead) and operational constraints (throughput, latency for streaming evals). The guidance is intended for evaluator engineers, model-onboarding owners, and probe stewards.

Goals:
- Reduce per-probe token cost by 20–60% without regressing key quality slices (intent accuracy, safety sentinel pass-rate).
- Provide an auditable compression workflow so that downstream continuous evals and shadow deployments use reproducible priming sets.
- Define metrics and an acceptance gate for compressed few-shot sets.

Background and tradeoffs:
Few-shot priming increases model context and improves alignment accuracy on many instruction tasks, but it also in...

title: Multimodal function-call onboarding contract and priming budget specification
preview:

This document defines the onboarding contract for services that expose multimodal function-call models to Redwood’s inference platform. text:
Summary:
This document defines the onboarding contract for services that expose multimodal function-call models to Redwood’s inference platform. It covers mandatory evaluation criteria, priming (few-shot) budget accounting, privacy constraints for example probes, and an operational roll-forward/rollback plan tailored to mixed-precision and quantized variants. The goal is to provide a deterministic acceptance gate for promotion from shadow to live, reduce regressions caused by priming-context changes, and standardize cost/quality trade-offs across tenants.

Scope:
- Applies to: any model suite that: accepts both text and images (or other non-text inputs) and supports the function-call API surface (structured tool outputs).
- Does NOT apply to: unimodal text-only models, embedding-only endpoints, or purely research validation artifacts.

Key definitions:
- Priming budget: the token + im...

title: Operator Interactive Regression Debugger — Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes the Operator Interactive Regression Debugger: a low-latency, human-in-the-loop protocol and toolkit for investigating production-quality regressions that show up in eval observability or customer signals. It complements automated bisect and probe tooling by enabling an operator or en...
text:
Summary:
This playbook describes the Operator Interactive Regression Debugger: a low-latency, human-in-the-loop protocol and toolkit for investigating production-quality regressions that show up in eval observability or customer signals. It complements automated bisect and probe tooling by enabling an operator or engineer to rapidly confirm causal factors, inspect intermediate artifacts (prompts, tokenization, KV cache traces), and run guarded micro-rollouts or targeted mitigations.

Scope:
- Intended for use by SREs, Applied ML engineers, and on-call evaluators responding to model quality regressions or severe fidelity shifts.
- Covers interactive investigation steps, CLI examples, automated helper scripts, telemetry checks, and safe rollback/mitigation actions.
- Not a replacement for automated bisect; use after initial triage if regression persists or is high-severity.

When to use...

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices an...
text:
Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices and regression detection sensitivity.

Goals:
- Maintain high-fidelity regression detection on high-risk slices (precision >= 0.90 on targeted failure classes).
- Reduce end-to-end eval cost per release by 40% (measured in GPU-minutes and token units).
- Provide reproducible, auditable sampling decisions and a transparent budget allocation for product/tenant workloads.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: applies to model onboarding, nightly and pre-release eval runs, and production regression triage workload mirroring. Does NOT replace small-sample human evaluation for subjective q...

title: Latency and Token-Budgeted Prompt Design Guide
preview:
Summary:
This guide defines a repeatable design and validation workflow for authoring prompts when you have hard latency or token-budget constraints. It covers principled prompt composition, token accounting, cache-priming patterns, integration tests for the eval harness, and rollout checks for model promotion. The ...
text:
Summary:
This guide defines a repeatable design and validation workflow for authoring prompts when you have hard latency or token-budget constraints. It covers principled prompt composition, token accounting, cache-priming patterns, integration tests for the eval harness, and rollout checks for model promotion. The target reader is engineers and applied-ML owners preparing prompts for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).

Scope and objectives:
- Scope: customer-facing text- and chat-style prompts used in production inference paths (single-turn and multi-turn). Does not cover multimodal binary payload formats.
- Objectives:
 - Ensure prompts meet latency SLOs and token budgets for cost predictability.
 - Maximize reuse of KV/prefix caches and enable safe quantized deployments.
 - Provide testable templates and an automated validation checklist for onboarding.

Key defi...

title: Promotion Readiness: Cross-Stage Synthetic Simulation & Quantization Safety Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a combined evaluation and safety protocol used to decide when a model artifact can be promoted from evaluation (canary/validation) into production routing. It coordinates three corridors of checks: 1) synthetic user simulation to validate behavioral and distributional stability, 2) qua...
text:
Summary

This playbook defines a combined evaluation and safety protocol used to decide when a model artifact can be promoted from evaluation (canary/validation) into production routing. It coordinates three corridors of checks: 1) synthetic user simulation to validate behavioral and distributional stability, 2) quantization & kernel-sensitivity safety checks for performance-preserving transforms, and 3) lightweight runtime smoke and canary procedures to exercise operational invariants. The goal is deterministic, reproducible promotion decisions tied to explicit pass/fail signals and rollback criteria.

Scope and Use Cases

- Applies to any model promotion that will be served by Redwood runtime variants where quantized or variant kernels (FP16/INT8/QLoRA-style) are in the promotion path.
- Intended for models with expected production throughput above 1 qps/customer or with regulatory/...

title: Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI) and Annotator Adaptation Manual
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI), describes a standardized annotator adaptation protocol, and provides operational runbooks for integrating QSPI into our promotion/evaluation pipeline for quantized artifacts. The goal is to detect, quantify, and remediate semantic or t...
text:
Summary:
This document defines the Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI), describes a standardized annotator adaptation protocol, and provides operational runbooks for integrating QSPI into our promotion/evaluation pipeline for quantized artifacts. The goal is to detect, quantify, and remediate semantic or task-specific signal loss introduced by quantization changes while minimizing annotation overhead and preserving production fidelity.

Why this matters:
- Quantization reduces cost but can change subtle tokenization/representation behavior that impacts downstream labelers, rerankers, and function-call correctness.
- Existing throughput-driven checks (latency/peak tokens) miss graded signal degradation affecting annotator agreement and quality SLAs.
- QSPI gives a single, explainable index and a fast path to adapt annotators and probe sets where signal loss is concentrated.

...

title: Service boundary evaluation and quantization migration checklist
preview:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements whi...
text:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements while preserving quality and observability contracts. This document ties together: eval-harness checks, prompt and benchmark selection, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, quantization profile validation, and regression triage steps.

Scope

- Applies to any model promotion that changes runtime numeric representation (FP16/FP32 -> INT8/PACT/4-bit) for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).
- Includes single-model migrations and model-variant rollouts where a quantized variant will be routed by policy.
- Excludes research-only artifacts and offline-only e...

title: Eval cost slice prioritization and resource budgeting
preview:
Summary

This document defines a prioritization and budgeting framework for running slice-driven evaluations in Redwood’s eval harness. It describes a deterministic scoring function for slice prioritization, recommended resource budgets for common evaluation classes (smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan),...
text:
Summary

This document defines a prioritization and budgeting framework for running slice-driven evaluations in Redwood’s eval harness. It describes a deterministic scoring function for slice prioritization, recommended resource budgets for common evaluation classes (smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan), integration points with quantization profiles and onboarding, and a triage fast-path for regression detection and mitigation.

Why this exists

- Eval budgets are finite: the eval harness runs thousands of slices across many models and releases. Uncontrolled execution causes high cost and slow feedback.
- We need consistent signal for promotion and regression triage while minimizing GPU/CPU spend.
- Quantization changes and heterogeneous deployments (private/dedicated) require different resource profiles; this doc standardizes budgeting and prioritization so teams follow p...

title: FP8/INT8 Regression Triage Playbook (Performance + Quality)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how to triage FP8/INT8 regressions detected during benchmarking, perf canary rollout, or customer reports. It covers:
- Common symptoms (performance, correctness, quality)
- First checks (flags, driver/CUDA, GPU clocks/thermals, cache state, shape support)

text:
## Purpose
This playbook describes how to triage FP8/INT8 regressions detected during benchmarking, perf canary rollout, or customer reports. It covers:
- Common symptoms (performance, correctness, quality)
- First checks (flags, driver/CUDA, GPU clocks/thermals, cache state, shape support)
- Dashboards and artifacts to consult
- A repeatable bisect strategy (kernel vs scheduler vs infra)
- Escalation paths and rollback criteria

**Audience:** Applied ML/Evals, Serving Runtime (Kernel + Scheduling), SRE/Infra.

---
## Definitions (used throughout)
- **TTFT**: time-to-first-token (ms)
- **TPOT**: time-per-output-token (ms/token) or decode latency per token
- **Prefill**: prompt processing phase
- **Decode**: generation phase
- **Mode**: `baseline` (FP16/BF16), `fp8`, `int8` (as tagged in runtime metrics)
- **Canary cohort**: region/model subset receiving new kernel-selection heuristics...

title: Optimization Suggestions (Beta): Guardrails, Safety Constraints, and Edge Cases
preview:
## Purpose
This document defines **guardrails** and **constraints** used by the Optimize Suggestions system (beta) when generating **batching** and **caching** recommendations. The goal is to ensure that suggestions:
- Are conservative and safe for production workloads.
- Avoid recommending changes that are likely t...
text:
## Purpose
This document defines **guardrails** and **constraints** used by the Optimize Suggestions system (beta) when generating **batching** and **caching** recommendations. The goal is to ensure that suggestions:
- Are conservative and safe for production workloads.
- Avoid recommending changes that are likely to violate latency/reliability SLOs.
- Are robust to telemetry gaps and known workload edge cases.
- Are explainable in the Console (“why we recommend this”, “what could go wrong”, and “how to apply safely”).

This doc is normative for suggestion generation logic. If a recommendation is blocked by a hard guardrail, it should not be emitted.

## Scope (beta)
In scope:
- **Batching suggestions:** tuning continuous batching parameters (e.g., max_batch_size, max_batch_delay_ms, target_tokens_per_batch) for a specific route/model.
- **Caching suggestions:** enabling or tuning pre...

title: Savings Estimation Validation Plan (Historical Telemetry) — Optimize Suggestions Beta
preview:
h1. Purpose
This document defines how we will validate the *savings estimator v0* for the Console “Optimization suggestions” beta (batching + caching only) using *historical telemetry*. The goal is to quantify estimation quality, characterize failure modes, and set *acceptance thresholds* for beta launch/expansion.

text:
h1. Purpose
This document defines how we will validate the *savings estimator v0* for the Console “Optimization suggestions” beta (batching + caching only) using *historical telemetry*. The goal is to quantify estimation quality, characterize failure modes, and set *acceptance thresholds* for beta launch/expansion.

This is *not* a customer commitment document. All outputs are “estimated savings” and must remain conservative and well-explained in-product.

h1. Scope
h2. In scope (beta)
* Savings estimation for:
 * *Batching suggestions* (continuous batching / max batch size / target batch latency window)
 * *Caching suggestions* (prefix/KV caching enablement and cache policy parameters)
* Metrics for:
 * *Cost impact* (token cost / GPU-seconds proxy where available)
 * *Latency impact* (p50/p95 deltas)
 * *Confidence inputs* (telemetry coverage, variance, stability)

h2. Out of scope ...

title: Regression triage playbook for Optimize configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how we investigate quality regressions that appear after an Optimize configuration change (batching policy updates, prefix/KV caching settings, or quantization profile switches). It is written for Applied ML oncall / Evals owners, with clear handoffs to Runtime and SRE.

This pl...
text:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how we investigate quality regressions that appear after an Optimize configuration change (batching policy updates, prefix/KV caching settings, or quantization profile switches). It is written for Applied ML oncall / Evals owners, with clear handoffs to Runtime and SRE.

This playbook is used in two primary situations:
- **A quality gate fails** (smoke or full gate) for a proposed Optimize change.
- **Online canary signals degrade** after a gated change is applied (e.g., increased refusals, task success drop, or customer-reported behavior change).

Non-goals:
- Deep performance tuning (covered by Runtime perf runbooks).
- Model onboarding or prompt set curation (covered by prompt-set ownership docs).

---

## Triage principles
1. **Treat output regressions as real until proven flaky.** Cost optimizations can produce subtle output changes; avoid di...

title: Cost Model Validation Harness (v1) — Golden Datasets, Replay, and Acceptance Criteria
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the validation harness for the Standard Cost Model (SCM) v1. The harness is the shared mechanism to:
- Verify correctness and determinism of per-request cost projections.
- Prevent regressions as schemas/runtime telemetry evolve.
- Quantify and monitor drift between predicted cost and ob...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the validation harness for the Standard Cost Model (SCM) v1. The harness is the shared mechanism to:
- Verify correctness and determinism of per-request cost projections.
- Prevent regressions as schemas/runtime telemetry evolve.
- Quantify and monitor drift between predicted cost and observed spend.
- Provide repeatable evidence for Console/Optimize adoption (and Finance alignment).

The harness is explicitly *not* a billing reconciliation system of record. It is a correctness/regression suite for the cost model spec + implementation.

## Scope
### In scope (v1)
- Golden fixtures for SCM inputs (request + deployment context + runtime/telemetry-derived primitives) and expected SCM outputs.
- Replay runner that executes the Cost Model API/service against fixtures in CI and nightly.
- Acceptance thresholds per output field and per aggregate view (prefill/dec...

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
preview:
Overview
========
This playbook describes the Shadow Orchestrator: an orchestrated, repeatable pipeline for running cross-modal (text + image + long-context) shadow tests against production traffic to detect regressions, quantization sensitivity, and early drift. The goal is to exercise the full serving stack and ev...
text:
Overview
========
This playbook describes the Shadow Orchestrator: an orchestrated, repeatable pipeline for running cross-modal (text + image + long-context) shadow tests against production traffic to detect regressions, quantization sensitivity, and early drift. The goal is to exercise the full serving stack and evaluation harness without impacting live responses.

Why this exists
---------------
- Catch subtle cross-modal regressions that only appear under production request composition (multimodal prompts, long context + tool calls).
- Measure behavior differences introduced by model promotions, quantization profiles, or runtime kernel changes.
- Provide a fast, audit-friendly path for risk-aware promotion decisions.

Scope
-----
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where shadowing of selective traffic is supported.
- Supports: text-only, text+image, and long-con...

title: Shadow scoring templates and rubrics (v1) — eval-harness spec
preview:
## Overview
This document defines the **initial (v1) rubric bundle** and **model-graded templates** used by **eval-harness** to score outputs produced by Shadow Traffic experiments.

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to candidate variants (model/version/quant/profile/prompt template) ...
text:
## Overview
This document defines the **initial (v1) rubric bundle** and **model-graded templates** used by **eval-harness** to score outputs produced by Shadow Traffic experiments.

Shadow Traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to candidate variants (model/version/quant/profile/prompt template) and scores shadow outputs asynchronously. The scoring goals are:
- Detect quality regressions **without impacting the user response path**.
- Produce **interpretable, statistically analyzable metrics** that can be compared across control vs shadow variants.
- Keep compute and storage overhead bounded (see guardrails in SRE docs).

This spec focuses on:
- The **rubric definitions** (format adherence, safety/refusal, instruction following)
- **Model-graded prompts** and response schemas
- **Calibration guidance** (thresholds, grader reliability, and power considerations)
- **...

title: Cascade Probe Resilience and Repair Playbook
preview:
SUMMARY
This page describes detection, emergency mitigation, and long-term prevention for cascading failures in the eval harness probe pipeline. We define failure modes that commonly cause large-scale evaluation regressions, provide an emergency runbook to restore safe continuity, and list durable design changes and...
text:
SUMMARY
This page describes detection, emergency mitigation, and long-term prevention for cascading failures in the eval harness probe pipeline. We define failure modes that commonly cause large-scale evaluation regressions, provide an emergency runbook to restore safe continuity, and list durable design changes and configuration standards to prevent recurrence.

Scope
- Applies to the eval-harness execution plane (probe generator, sequencer, shadow router, and annotator ingestion queue).
- Includes interactions with: quantized shadow artifacts, KV cache warmup probes, and external annotator workers.
- Does NOT cover model training pipeline bugs or user-facing product incidents unless they are caused by an eval probe cascade.

Why this matters
- A single overloaded probe generator or stalled annotation queue can amplify into systemic false-positive regressions, wasted compute, and pro...

title: Contrastive probe assembly and sweeping guidelines
preview:
Overview

This guideline defines a contrastive, instrumented approach to assembling probe families and sweeping them through the eval mesh so we can detect subtle regressions, quantization sensitivity, and prompt coverage gaps before promoting model artifacts to production. It ties together probe fabrication, priori...
text:
Overview

This guideline defines a contrastive, instrumented approach to assembling probe families and sweeping them through the eval mesh so we can detect subtle regressions, quantization sensitivity, and prompt coverage gaps before promoting model artifacts to production. It ties together probe fabrication, priority-led scheduling, quantization-aware sampling, and rapid regression triage hooks into a single operational playbook.

When to use this document

- New model onboarding where prompt families are large or heterogeneous
- Quantization or mixed-precision migration projects that require targeted sensitivity checks
- Routine sweep windows for high-risk services (high revenue or high regulatory sensitivity)
- After a rollout that impacted tail behavior or embedding stability

Terminology

- probe family: a group of related prompts and expected outputs designed to exercise a behav...

title: Quantization cost modeling notes (Standard Cost Model v1)
preview:
h1. Summary
This page defines how we parameterize the *cost impact* of quantization (throughput + memory) and how we represent *quality constraints/guardrails* so Quantization Profiles can be used consistently by the Standard Cost Model v1 and the Cost Model API.

The intent is not to prescribe which quantization me...
text:
h1. Summary
This page defines how we parameterize the *cost impact* of quantization (throughput + memory) and how we represent *quality constraints/guardrails* so Quantization Profiles can be used consistently by the Standard Cost Model v1 and the Cost Model API.

The intent is not to prescribe which quantization method to use for a given model; it is to ensure that when a model advertises a quantization profile (e.g., fp16, fp8, int8, int4-awq), *Console* and *Optimize* see the same cost primitives and semantics.

h2. Scope
*In scope (v1):*
* Quantization profile taxonomy and stable identifiers
* Required profile parameters to model cost impact (throughput, memory, kernel/runtime compatibility)
* Representation of uncertainty in projections (confidence bands) for Optimize what-if
* Mapping into the Standard Cost Model spec: inputs (profile metadata) and outputs (quantization deltas)
...

title: Multi-tenant Canary Eval: Privacy Anchoring & Probe Design Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a reproducible, privacy-preserving approach for running multi-tenant canary evaluations of model and serving changes. It describes probe design, anchoring strategies, acceptance criteria, telemetry schema, and rollback procedures tailored for shared evaluator fleets and tenan...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines a reproducible, privacy-preserving approach for running multi-tenant canary evaluations of model and serving changes. It describes probe design, anchoring strategies, acceptance criteria, telemetry schema, and rollback procedures tailored for shared evaluator fleets and tenant isolation constraints.

Why this doc exists
- Multi-tenant canaries enable fast feedback but increase privacy risk and cross-tenant interference.
- We need deterministic, auditable probes that exercise production traffic slices while bounding leakage and differential privacy (DP) exposure.
- This playbook standardizes the orchestration (evaluator-orch, canary-operator), probe provenance (probe-store), and decision gates used by model promotion and runtime rollouts.

Scope and audience
- Audience: Applied ML engineers, evaluation owners, SREs operating the evaluator fleet,...

title: Perf Canary: Synthetic Prompt Distribution (v0.3)
preview:
## Overview
This page defines the **synthetic prompt distribution** used by the production performance canary ("perf-canary"). The canary exists to detect **p95 latency regressions** and **tokens/sec drops** per model (optionally per region/tier/SKU) with minimal noise and zero customer data.

The prompt distributio...
text:
## Overview
This page defines the **synthetic prompt distribution** used by the production performance canary ("perf-canary"). The canary exists to detect **p95 latency regressions** and **tokens/sec drops** per model (optionally per region/tier/SKU) with minimal noise and zero customer data.

The prompt distribution is intentionally **shape-focused** (token lengths, prefill/decode mix, and request patterns) and uses **template-generated, non-sensitive content**.

**Version:** v0.3 (current)

---

## Goals
1. Provide a stable, reproducible workload that approximates typical production request shapes:
 - Short chat turns
 - Medium instruction-style requests
 - Longer context (RAG-like) inputs
2. Exercise runtime performance characteristics that commonly regress:
 - Prefill attention (prompt length sensitivity)
 - Decode loop efficiency (generation length sensitivity)
 - Scheduler/queue...

title: Service Slice Provenance and Regression Huntbook
preview:

This document codifies a repeatable hunt-and-triage workflow for production-quality regressions that manifest at the service-slice level. It focuses on building a provenance-first investigation flow: assemble evidence, identify likely change vectors (code/runtime/model/quant/priming), run a targeted probe ...
text:
Overview

This document codifies a repeatable hunt-and-triage workflow for production-quality regressions that manifest at the service-slice level. It focuses on building a provenance-first investigation flow: assemble evidence, identify likely change vectors (code/runtime/model/quant/priming), run a targeted probe fabricator to reproduce the slice, and produce safe remediation or rollback actions. Designed for on-call evaluators, ML SREs, and model owners.

Goals
- Reduce mean-time-to-detect-and-resolve for slice-level regressions by 50% for high-priority services.
- Standardize a provenance ledger for each regression investigation to enable auditability and postmortem learning.
- Provide tooling patterns for low-cost synthetic probe generation that preserves signal while minimizing eval spend.

Scope and definitions
- Service slice: A tagged combination of tenant, route, prompt-fami...

title: Quantization profile versioning, evaluation, promotion, and rollback
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood evaluates, promotes, and rolls back **quantization profiles** when they are managed as **versioned Optimization Config** objects.

Quantization changes are uniquely risky: they can improve cost/latency while silently degrading output quality. Our process therefore combines:

text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood evaluates, promotes, and rolls back **quantization profiles** when they are managed as **versioned Optimization Config** objects.

Quantization changes are uniquely risky: they can improve cost/latency while silently degrading output quality. Our process therefore combines:
- Production safety gates (5xx, p95/p99 latency, saturation), and
- **Quality gates** (eval harness) specific to quantization.

This doc is the Applied ML “source of truth” for:
- What constitutes a quantization profile version
- Which evals/prompt sets run during canary and promotion
- Regression thresholds and how to interpret them
- Rollback semantics and post-rollback verification

## Audience
- Applied ML / Evals engineers operating quant profile changes
- Platform engineers integrating quality gates into safe apply
- SRE/on-call responding to quant-related incidents
- ...

title: Evaluation Portfolio Prioritization Framework

text:
Purpose:
This document defines a lightweight, repeatable framework for maintaining and prioritizing Redwood's evaluation portfolio across eval-harness runs, prompt-benchmarks, onboarding checks, quantization profiles, and regression triage exercises. The goal is to ensure eval work is aligned to product impact, risk, and operational cost so we run the right tests at the right cadence and react quickly to detected regressions.

Scope:
- Applies to all evaluation activities owned by Applied ML & Evals, Model Onboarding, and Runtime teams.
- Covers both offline (batch) evals and lightweight online/serving simulations.
- Does not replace specific test implementations or labeling processes; it provides a prioritization and scheduling layer.

Key definitions:
- Evaluation asset: any runnable test, prompt set, or benchmark (examples: long-context-robustness suite, prompt-coverage matrix, qua...

title: Federated Probe Prioritization and Orchestration Playbook
preview:
Overview:
This playbook defines a federated probe orchestration pattern and a deterministic prioritization schema for the Redwood eval harness. It is intended to align evaluation traffic across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments (including VPC/on-prem shadowing) so that scarce eval capacity is used to maximi...
text:
Overview:
This playbook defines a federated probe orchestration pattern and a deterministic prioritization schema for the Redwood eval harness. It is intended to align evaluation traffic across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments (including VPC/on-prem shadowing) so that scarce eval capacity is used to maximize signal for regression detection, quantization stability, and rollout safety while respecting cost and privacy constraints.

Scope and motivation:
- Scope: evaluation probes executed by the central eval mesh and by regional probe farms. Includes both synthetic probes (generated test cases) and production-derived probes (anonymized slices).
- Motivation: rising eval volume, heterogeneous capacity (quantized vs FP16 runtime), and mixed confidentiality requirements have caused uneven coverage. We need a single prioritization policy plus lightweight orchestration controls tha...

title: Optimize Quality Gates: Eval-Harness Integration Contracts (v1)
preview:
## Summary

The contract covers:

text:
## Summary
This page defines the interface contract between **Optimize** (producer of proposed serving config diffs) and the **Eval Harness** (runner of offline quality evaluations) for **quality guardrails gating**.

The contract covers:
- Inputs required to run a gate (config diff, baseline, tier, prompt sets, model/runtime metadata)
- Output schema (per-suite scores, deltas vs baseline, confidence/variance metadata, artifact pointers)
- State machine + polling semantics
- SLAs (latency, availability), retry/idempotency rules, and cancellation
- Versioning and backwards compatibility expectations

This contract is for **Hosted API** gating (initial rollout). Dedicated/Private extensions should follow the same shapes but may use different auth and artifact backends.

---

## Glossary
- **Gate run**: An eval-harness execution comparing a *candidate* configuration to a *baseline* confi...

title: Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework
preview:
Overview

This document defines the Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework (PBAEF), an evaluation harness designed to: 1) detect persona-conditional bias and safety regressions, 2) provide stable anchor baselines for longitudinal calibration, and 3) integrate with Redwood’s eval orchestration for automated t...
text:
Overview

This document defines the Persona Bias Probing and Anchor Eval Framework (PBAEF), an evaluation harness designed to: 1) detect persona-conditional bias and safety regressions, 2) provide stable anchor baselines for longitudinal calibration, and 3) integrate with Redwood’s eval orchestration for automated triage and alerting. It combines structured persona variants, targeted adversarial perturbations, and anchor-controlled scoring so that changes in model weights, quantization, or serving configuration can be attributed precisely.

Goals and scope

- Primary goals:
 - Surface persona-dependent failure modes (bias, toxicity, hallucination) across representative workloads.
 - Provide anchor baselines that remain stable across model promotions and deployments to detect relative regressions.
 - Integrate with regression triage playbooks to shorten mean time to detect and resolve ...

title: Evaluation Asset Registry and Evaluator Service Contract
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The E...
text:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The EAR + ESC are intended to reduce duplicated probe creation, improve reproducibility, and provide clear operational guarantees for model onboarding, quantization, and regression triage workflows.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
 - Provide a single, queryable registry of evaluation assets (probe sets, slice tags, sample annotations, golden outputs, evaluator configs, telemetry fingerprints, quant-compatibility reports).
 - Standardize metadata such that evaluators, CI, and the serving runtime can make deterministic promotion/rollback decisions.
 - Define SLOs and behavioral exp...

title: SLO Tier PromptSet Selection (Fast / Standard / Cost-Efficient)
preview:
## Overview
This page defines how Redwood selects and weights prompt sets used to validate the **Fast**, **Standard**, and **Cost-Efficient** latency SLO tiers for workload-aware batching defaults.

The prompt sets here are used by the serving runtime perf suite and canary gates to answer:
- Do the tiered batching p...
text:
## Overview
This page defines how Redwood selects and weights prompt sets used to validate the **Fast**, **Standard**, and **Cost-Efficient** latency SLO tiers for workload-aware batching defaults.

The prompt sets here are used by the serving runtime perf suite and canary gates to answer:
- Do the tiered batching profiles meet published latency targets (p95/p99 where applicable)?
- Do we improve throughput and $/1K tokens without changing request semantics?
- Do results generalize to real production traffic (within known limitations)?

This doc is intentionally about **prompt selection/weighting** (task mix + length mix + streaming mix). For execution details (concurrency ramps, warmup, pass/fail gates), see the benchmark methodology page.

## Scope
In scope:
- Prompt set taxonomy (chat vs summarization vs code, etc.)
- Tier-specific weighting and why it differs by tier
- Prompt-leng...

text:
Overview

Purpose: Provide a standardized, workload-driven quantization autotuning workflow plus safety validators and rollout criteria for model serving at Redwood. This doc codifies the tests, thresholds, and fallback policies to use when generating per-workload quantization profiles and before promoting profiles to production.

Scope
- Applies to all encoder-decoder and decoder-only language models onboarded to Redwood Dedicated and Redwood Private (hosted inference workflows use a subset of these checks).
- Targets weight-only and mixed precision quantization (8-bit, 6-bit, and FP8 experimental profiles) and int8 dynamic/aware flows.
- Does not replace model-level release notes or licensing checks — coordinates with model-onboarding owners.

Definitions
- Quant profile: Serialized configuration (YAML) that describes per-layer precision, grouped quantization settings, and kernel hi...

title: Model warmup probe and priming playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a standardized approach for warming and priming models, caches, and serving runtime state before or during rollout. It covers objectives, probe types, probe generation, quantization-aware warmups, monitoring signals, and a regression triage fast-path for warmup-induced failures. Use th...
text:
Summary

This playbook defines a standardized approach for warming and priming models, caches, and serving runtime state before or during rollout. It covers objectives, probe types, probe generation, quantization-aware warmups, monitoring signals, and a regression triage fast-path for warmup-induced failures. Use this doc for: model onboarding, canary rollout preparations, private/dedicated deployment warmups, and automated preflight checks in the eval harness.

Goals and scope

- Reduce first-request tail latency and token p99 inflation during traffic ramp.
- Stabilize KV cache and attention-key/value layout effects so price/quality regressions are reproducible.
- Provide reproducible probe suites that exercise: short-context generations, long-context KV growth, streaming function-calls, and quantized kernel paths.
- Applicable to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Owner an...

title: Noise Sweep & Quantization Sanity Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines a reproducible, low-friction process for running a contextual noise sweep and performing quantization sanity checks before promoting quantized artifacts into shadow or production. The goal is to detect subtle degradation modes that standard functional tests miss (e.g., prompt sensitivi...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines a reproducible, low-friction process for running a contextual noise sweep and performing quantization sanity checks before promoting quantized artifacts into shadow or production. The goal is to detect subtle degradation modes that standard functional tests miss (e.g., prompt sensitivity, prefix cache corruption, and token-level coherence regressions) and to provide deterministic roll-forward/rollback criteria tied to telemetry and canary gates.

Scope:
- Applies to all models targeting mixed- or int8-based quant profiles intended for Dedicated/Private serving.
- Intended for use by Applied ML, Model Onboarding, and Runtime teams during pre-promotion validation and canary rollout.

Owner and stakeholders:
- Playbook owner: Applied ML — Model Reliability (owner: Priya Nair)
- Stakeholders: Serving Runtime, Observability, Product QA, and Security (for priv...

title: Longtail cost-fidelity heatmap and action matrix
preview:
Summary:
This playbook defines a repeatable process and automation primitives to compute a cost vs. fidelity heatmap for long‑tail workload slices, map heatmap quadrants to concrete actions, and integrate those actions into eval harness pipelines and rollout automation. The goal is to prioritize limited human and co...
text:
Summary:
This playbook defines a repeatable process and automation primitives to compute a cost vs. fidelity heatmap for long‑tail workload slices, map heatmap quadrants to concrete actions, and integrate those actions into eval harness pipelines and rollout automation. The goal is to prioritize limited human and compute effort by focusing on slices that will yield the highest ROI for remediation or rollback.

Scope:
- Applies to model promotions and post‑deployment regressions across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private modes.
- Focused on long‑tail slices (rare intents, regional/localized language, multimodal edge cases) that historically contribute >5% of fidelity regressions but <20% of total traffic.

Definitions and key signals:
- Slice: a labeled subset of traffic defined by prompt family, locale, or function call usage.
- Fidelity delta (ΔF): change in a slice’s quality metric aggre...

title: Regression Impact Index and Prioritization Framework
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Regression Impact Index (RII), a lightweight but rigorous scoring system that consolidates signal-level regressions into a single numeric index used to prioritize investigations and rollbacks. The RII is intended for use by the eval, inference, and SRE teams during post-deploy moni...
text:
Summary:
This document defines the Regression Impact Index (RII), a lightweight but rigorous scoring system that consolidates signal-level regressions into a single numeric index used to prioritize investigations and rollbacks. The RII is intended for use by the eval, inference, and SRE teams during post-deploy monitoring, regression triage, and model promotion decisions.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
 - Provide a repeatable, explainable numeric index (0-100) that summarizes the business and technical impact of a model regression.
 - Standardize prioritization across teams and deployment modes (hosted, dedicated, private).
 - Drive consistent action thresholds and SLAs for response.
- Non-goals:
 - Replace domain-specific human judgment. RII is a triage tool, not an absolute gate.
 - Replace longer-form root cause analysis (RCA) or formal postmortems.

Definitions:
- Regression: any ...

text:
## Purpose
This document defines the **formal, reproducible methodology** for Redwood Inference’s public-facing **Latency-per-Dollar** benchmark report ("Benchmark Report v1"). It is the source-of-truth for:
- Workload definitions (input/output token shapes and request mixes)
- Prompt sets and sampling rules
- Concurrency and load generation
- Warmup and stability gates
- Caching and batching policy
- Metrics and aggregation (including Latency/$ normalization)
- Exclusions and fairness constraints

This methodology is written to support:
1) internal replication and audits, 2) consistent cross-region runs, and 3) legal substantiation for any public claims.

## Scope
**In scope**
- Redwood Hosted API benchmarks across supported public regions
- Redwood Dedicated/Private benchmarks when explicitly tagged as such in the run manifest
- Optional contextual baselines (when approved) with cle...

title: Stochastic Eval Replay and Determinism Protocol
preview:
Summary
-------
This document defines the protocol and runbook for capturing, replaying, and triaging nondeterministic behavior observed in evaluation harness runs. It codifies artifact capture, deterministic-mode execution, tolerance thresholds, and escalation steps so engineering and applied ML teams can reliably ...
text:
Summary
-------
This document defines the protocol and runbook for capturing, replaying, and triaging nondeterministic behavior observed in evaluation harness runs. It codifies artifact capture, deterministic-mode execution, tolerance thresholds, and escalation steps so engineering and applied ML teams can reliably diagnose and reduce stochastic divergence between runs.

Scope
-----
- Applies to all automated and manual eval harness runs used for production model certification, A/B tests, and regression detection.
- Covers sources of nondeterminism including sampling configs (temperature, top_k/top_p), tokenizer differences, runtime kernel scheduling, batch coalescing, KV cache state, and hardware/driver variance.
- Does NOT cover data-collection-level label noise or human annotation variance (see separate QA guidelines).

Goals
-----
- Make a failing or flaky eval reproducible within...

title: Experiment Quality Evals Integration (Route Experiments)
preview:
## Overview
This document defines how Redwood attaches quality evaluation (eval) signals to route-level experiment variants and surfaces them as first-class metrics in Console Rollouts.

The goal is to make “quality” a measurable guardrail alongside latency, cost, and errors, without requiring customers to bring cus...
text:
## Overview
This document defines how Redwood attaches quality evaluation (eval) signals to route-level experiment variants and surfaces them as first-class metrics in Console Rollouts.

The goal is to make “quality” a measurable guardrail alongside latency, cost, and errors, without requiring customers to bring custom metrics in the initial release.

This design covers:
- How we generate and attribute eval score streams to {route, experiment_id, variant_id}.
- Sampling and prompt-set execution strategy so variant comparisons are meaningful.
- Score normalization so metrics are comparable across prompt sets and over time.
- Quality regression guardrails (detection + alerting + optional auto-stop integration).

## Goals
1) Provide an attributed, near-real-time “quality score” time series for each experiment variant.
2) Enable guardrails for quality regressions with clear defaults, and ...

title: Quantization Regression Thresholds (Quality Policy)
preview:

This page defines Redwood Inference’s quality policy for *quantization-related changes* (e.g., enabling a new Quantization Profile, updating profile artifacts, changing kernel selection for quantized paths). It specifies:
- Required evaluation suites and minimum coverage

text:
## Purpose
This page defines Redwood Inference’s quality policy for *quantization-related changes* (e.g., enabling a new Quantization Profile, updating profile artifacts, changing kernel selection for quantized paths). It specifies:
- Required evaluation suites and minimum coverage
- Acceptable regression thresholds (by task family)
- Statistical methods and pass/fail rules
- Automatic block criteria (pre-publish) and automatic rollback triggers (during canary/beta)

The intent is to reduce cost/token while keeping output quality within agreed bounds and ensuring regressions are detected quickly, are attributable, and are reversible.

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Any new or updated quantization profile intended for publication in the Quantization Profile Library
- Any runtime or kernel-path change that affects quantized execution correctness or output distribution

title: Quantization Profile Library (QPL) — Overview
preview:
h1. Quantization Profile Library (QPL) — Overview

The Quantization Profile Library (QPL) is Redwood Inference’s opt-in mechanism for publishing *per-model quantization profiles* and allowing customers to enable them *at the route level* (and optionally by region/deployment tier) to reduce inference cost while maint...
text:
h1. Quantization Profile Library (QPL) — Overview

The Quantization Profile Library (QPL) is Redwood Inference’s opt-in mechanism for publishing *per-model quantization profiles* and allowing customers to enable them *at the route level* (and optionally by region/deployment tier) to reduce inference cost while maintaining quality and latency SLOs.

This page defines the goals, terminology, lifecycle, and ownership boundaries across Applied ML, Serving Runtime, and Platform.

---

h2. Goals (v1)

* Publish a *stable, versioned* quantization-profile schema per model variant.
* Allow *safe, explicit enablement* via route config (no silent changes / no default-on behavior).
* Guarantee *deterministic selection and application* of an approved profile in the serving runtime.
* Provide *evaluation and quality guardrails* that prevent regressions from reaching GA routes.
* Support staged roll...

Summary

This guide documents a practical, repeatable process to build a prompt coverage matrix, detect and annotate semantic/lexical drift, and run repair/mitigation steps tied into Redwood's eval harness and onboarding pipelines. It is intended for Applied ML engineers, model-onboarding owners, and SREs who operate regression detection and remediation workflows.

Scope

- Presents a template coverage matrix and annotation schema.
- Shows how to integrate checks into eval-runner pipelines and CI gating.
- Describes triage and repair playbook for drift regressions (quality and performance).
- Notes quantization-aware checks and data requirements for robust comparisons.

text:
## Purpose
This playbook defines the standard process for running **route-level A/B evaluations** comparing:
- **Baseline:** default (non-profile) inference settings
- **Treatment:** a specific **Quantization Profile Library** profile enabled at the **route** level

The goal is to validate **quality**, **latency**, **stability**, and **cost/token** impacts before enabling a quantization profile for customer traffic and before publishing/promoting a profile version.

This is the canonical procedure used for:
- Applied ML validation prior to publishing/promoting a profile
- Platform/Console canary readiness checks for design partners
- SRE/Release gating checks prior to expanding rollout percentage

---

## Definitions
**Route:** A Redwood control-plane configuration unit used by customers to send inference traffic (e.g., `routes/chat-prod`).

title: Quant profile requirements for Hosted API catalog models
preview:
# Overview
This page defines the minimum set of quantization profiles and validation checks required for a model to be eligible for the Hosted API catalog.

# Why we require multiple profiles
Hosted traffic varies significantly by latency sensitivity and context length. Having pre-validated quantization profiles ens...
text:
# Overview
This page defines the minimum set of quantization profiles and validation checks required for a model to be eligible for the Hosted API catalog.

# Why we require multiple profiles
Hosted traffic varies significantly by latency sensitivity and context length. Having pre-validated quantization profiles ensures we can route cost-sensitive workloads without surprise quality regressions.

# Required profiles (per model)
For every new catalog model, you must produce and validate *at least* the following profiles:
- `fp16` (reference)
- `int8-weight-only` (cost-optimized)
- `int4-weight-only` (when supported by kernels/hardware; if not supported, document as N/A with Runtime owner signoff)

# Validation checks (required)
Each profile must pass:
1. **Correctness smoke** (Owner: Serving Runtime)
 - Generate determinism check (same seed, same output distribution tolerance)
 - Tokeni...

This is used by the Unit Economics dashboard (internal) and by Applied ML / ...
text:
## Overview
This page defines the canonical way to:
1) Map **quantization profiles** to **model identifiers** in telemetry, and
2) Track the impact of quantization on **blended $/1M output tokens**, **latency**, and **quality guardrails**.

This is used by the Unit Economics dashboard (internal) and by Applied ML / Runtime teams to safely roll out new quantization profiles without creating attribution gaps or high-cardinality telemetry.

**Primary outcomes**
- Quantization experiments and rollouts are attributable in metering and aggregation (no “unknown profile” buckets except for explicitly allowed fallbacks).
- Cost improvements are measurable and comparable across time, region, and customer tier.
- Quality regressions are caught via guardrails tied to prompt sets/evals before broad rollout.

- Model metadata + allowed values: Quentin Harris

# Overview
This page defines the required evaluation gates for launching a new model into the Hosted API model catalog. The goal is to ensure we can safely serve the model, meet product quality expectations, and detect regressions after launch.

# Ownership and signoff
- Gate owner (quality): Applied ML (Onboarding + Evals)
- Required signoff: Applied ML + Product (for model positioning)
- Optional signoff: Customer Success (if a launch is for a named account)

# Required evaluation suites
All Hosted API catalog launches must pass *all* suites below. Each suite must be run on the candidate model build (exact weights + tokenizer + runtime config) and logged in the eval harness.

text:
# Purpose
This page describes the *intake* process for launching a new third‑party LLM in the Redwood Hosted API model catalog. It defines what the requester must provide, who owns each step, and what artifacts are required before engineering work starts.

# When to use
Use this process for any new entry in the Hosted API model catalog, including:
- New upstream model families (e.g., Llama, Qwen, Mistral)
- New sizes or instruction variants
- New upstream releases that change tokenizer, weights, or license

# Roles
- Requester: typically Product or Solutions Engineering
- Intake owner: Applied ML Onboarding (Applied ML)
- Runtime owner: Serving Runtime
- Release owner: Release Engineering
- Security gate owner: Security & Compliance
- Docs owner: DevEx / Docs

# Intake ticket and required fields
Create a Linear ticket in *ENG* with label `model-onboarding` and include the following fi...
